accepted_009.assessment:
This paper presents a clean and well-motivated shift from greedy next-best-view to learned next-best-path via a unified value-and-obstacle prediction model. The approach is technically sound, easy to implement, and empirically effective, with particularly compelling gains on a challenging new dataset. The dataset contribution itself is valuable and, if released as promised with generation tools, will help the community probe long-horizon exploration strategies under varied topologies. The main reservation is the limited comparison to recent long-horizon/global planners that also aim to escape local minima (e.g., hierarchical or 3DGS-based methods, and RL goal policies). While the experiments convincingly demonstrate superiority over NBV and FBE baselines, stronger baselines would substantiate the “state-of-the-art” claim more firmly. Despite this, I find the combination of a novel and practical modeling idea, solid ablations, and a potentially impactful dataset to meet the bar for ICLR. I recommend a weak accept, with encouragement to broaden comparisons, report runtime, and add robustness studies to strengthen the camera-ready.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_051.assessment:
This work tackles an important and underexplored setting—fully-inductive node classification across arbitrary feature and label spaces—by combining analytic LinearGNN predictors with a carefully designed, permutation- and dimension-robust attention mechanism. The approach is simple, principled, and efficient, and the empirical study over 31 datasets is broad and generally supportive, with informative ablations and visualizations. That said, several aspects warrant clarification or strengthening: numerical stability and regularization for the pseudoinverse, a more precise complexity analysis, statistical significance of average improvements, and additional or alternative baselines (regularized training-free methods, APPNP, and where possible, cross-graph “one-for-all” models or text-attributed pipelines). Overall, I find the problem and the proposed solution compelling, and with the above clarifications and additional analyses, I believe this work would be a valuable contribution to ICLR. I lean toward acceptance.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_057.assessment:
This paper makes a solid and timely contribution by extending “learning without mixing” guarantees to VAR(p) with clear, interpretable rates and by highlighting gains under low-rank structure and misspecified contexts. The operator-based formulation and uniform martingale concentration are technically interesting and address real obstacles that preclude a naive companion-form reduction. The main limitations are the reliance on a known operator-norm bound (or a stronger NT regime for OLS), the potentially hidden p-dependence through D, and the restrictive misspecification assumption. Empirical validation is also light. Overall, I view the work as an advance in non-asymptotic system identification for long-context models, with high potential impact, and recommend acceptance after encouraging the authors to (i) elevate the unconstrained OLS statement to the main text, (ii) clarify the scaling of D with p via concrete examples, and (iii) strengthen empirical illustrations, especially for misspecification and low-rank benefits.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_045.assessment:
This paper offers a clear and conceptually appealing reframing of sparsified/Dropout training as optimization over an explicitly sketched objective, together with solid inheritance properties and SGD-style convergence guarantees that explain empirical heuristics (e.g., smaller learning rates under higher sparsity and the potential for exact convergence in interpolated regimes). The distributed extension is timely and relevant. However, the theory focuses on linear, unbiased sketches and employs pessimistic constants (notably L_S^max), limiting practical sharpness and coverage of widely used compressors. The experiments, while suggestive, are too limited in scope and comparative depth to establish practical significance at ICLR standards. With strengthened empirical validation (larger-scale, strong baselines, runtime/communication metrics) and tighter analyses (e.g., problem-dependent bounds), the work could make a substantial impact. As it stands, I view this as a promising and insightful conceptual contribution with room to mature; my recommendation is borderline leaning toward rejection primarily due to experimental and comparative gaps, and the conservativeness of key constants.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_003.assessment:
This paper introduces a refreshing and practically motivated perspective on text-to-3D: treating 3D surfaces as images via multi-chart geometry images and leveraging a frozen 2D prior through collaborative control to generate textured, part-separable meshes quickly and without specialized 3D architectures. The approach is conceptually elegant, the preprocessing pipeline is sensible, and the qualitative results are promising, especially the semantic separability and IPAdapter compatibility. However, the current empirical validation is largely qualitative and lacks the breadth and rigor expected at ICLR: there are no quantitative comparisons against strong baselines, no standardized evaluations of text alignment or 3D consistency, no mesh-quality measurements, limited ablations, and no substantiated runtime profiling. Additionally, key practical limitations (seam cracks, absence of full PBR channels, polygon budget control) constrain immediate applicability. With stronger experimental evidence and engineering of the stitching/material channels, the method could be impactful; in its current form, I lean toward rejection, while recognizing the novelty and potential of the core idea.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_021.assessment:
This paper tackles an important and timely problem—interpretable integration of asynchronous, multi-regional neural recordings—using a novel and well-motivated combination of sparse, region-structured ensemble discovery and a shared dictionary of sub-circuit interactions that vary over time and across sessions. The synthetic experiments are thorough and compelling, and the real-data case studies yield interpretable motifs with plausible neuroscientific significance and promising predictive signals. The main weaknesses are (i) under-specified optimization and identifiability details; (ii) limited quantitative comparisons against strong baselines on real data; and (iii) the need for more rigorous evaluations of cross-session alignment and transfer. Despite these, the conceptual contribution is strong and the practical results are encouraging. With clarifications and additional real-data baselines, this work would make a valuable, high-impact contribution to the ICLR community. I lean toward a weak accept.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_063.assessment:
This paper tackles an important problem—providing fine-grained, interpretable reward signals for LVLMs—and proposes a practical token-level RM with several compelling applications (hallucination diagnostics, guided self-correction, lightweight backbone improvements, and faster human annotation). The architectural integration is simple and broadly usable, and the ablations on the projection/decoder layers are informative. However, the central weakness is the supervision pipeline: negative token labels are derived from minimal textual perturbations without robust image-grounded verification, so the reward can conflate reference similarity with visual faithfulness. This undermines both the validity of TLDR as a stand-alone hallucination evaluator and the interpretability of “automatic likelihood optimization” gains. Empirical validation is suggestive but not decisive: response-level gains over a naive RM are modest, self-correction and human studies are small or potentially confounded, and comparisons against closely related fine-grained LVLM reward/evaluation methods are missing. With stronger external validation (CHAIR/POPE/FaithScore), tighter human audits, and improved perturbation labeling grounded in the image, TLDR could become a valuable tool. In its current form, I view the contribution as promising but not yet at the level of rigor and comparative validation expected for ICLR acceptance.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_081.assessment:
ShortcutsBench is a timely and valuable contribution: it assembles a realistic, human-authored workflow corpus from Apple Shortcuts, pairs it with grounded queries, and evaluates core agent abilities that remain under-tested—selection under long horizons, structured parameter filling, and explicit information-elicitation behavior. The experimental breadth and analyses convincingly show that planning/selection is the principal bottleneck and that “ask awareness” is notably weak across models. The main limitations are the lack of end-to-end execution metrics, reliance on GPT-generated queries, constrained candidate sets (no retrieval track), and a somewhat coarse measure for request recognition. Related work coverage misses several close contemporaries (MCP-Bench, ToolDial, WorkflowLLM), and some methodological details (distractor sampling, control-flow handling) need clarification. Overall, I find the benchmark impactful and well-motivated, with moderate methodological gaps that can be addressed in revisions. I lean toward a weak accept given the resource’s potential value to the community and the clarity of the empirical insights, contingent on improved discussion of limitations, added ablations (especially on candidate-set composition), and clearer positioning relative to closely related benchmarks.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_069.assessment:
This paper presents a pragmatic, clean SSL framework for motion prediction that deliberately avoids architectural entanglement and single-dataset constraints. While the individual components (temporal contrastive learning, trajectory reconstruction, standardization for dataset pooling) are not conceptually new, the combined recipe is well-motivated for this domain and empirically useful. The experiments are broad across strong backbones and show consistent improvements, and the ablations are informative. The main limitations lie in the modest novelty, the missing empirical coverage for raster backbones and an additional downstream dataset (e.g., WOMD), and limited comparisons to more recent SSL baselines. Overall, I view this as a solid, application-driven contribution that would be valuable to the motion forecasting community—particularly for practitioners seeking plug-and-play SSL that scales with data—despite being less theoretically or algorithmically groundbreaking. I lean toward a weak accept, contingent on clarifications and, if possible, inclusion of an additional downstream dataset or a raster-based backbone result to strengthen the model-agnostic and dataset-agnostic claims.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_075.assessment:
This paper offers a compelling empirical observation—that submodules of LLMs exhibit stronger linearity than the full model—and turns it into a simple, training-free, module-wise merging algorithm with small-data closed-form solves. The idea is timely and practically useful, and the gains over task arithmetic and DARE on Llama-2-7B/13B across three task domains are encouraging. However, the current version omits key, closely related baselines (LOT Merging, Chain of Merges/RegMean-style activation regression) and does not address a well-known pitfall of independent layer mergers—merging covariate shift—which likely limits performance and generality. Some clarity issues (notably the Non-linearity Score typo and missing closed-form details) and a relatively narrow evaluation suite further temper confidence. With added comparisons to state-of-the-art layer-wise training-free methods, a sequential activation-aware variant, broader benchmarks, and corrected/exposed derivations, this work could become a strong contribution. As it stands, I view it as promising but not yet at the bar of a top-tier venue; I encourage a revision addressing the above points.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_087.assessment:
This is a valuable and timely dataset contribution that squarely targets a central blocker in ICRL research: the lack of large, diverse, and properly structured learning histories across many tasks. The paper is well-motivated, the engineering is strong and transparent, and the preliminary benchmarks are sufficient to show both the dataset’s utility and the current limitations of standard methods. The main reservations are the narrow domain scope, the approximate nature of expert relabeling and its unproven usefulness for DPT-like training in POMDPs, the limited baseline coverage, and the absence of standardized evaluation splits and ablations that would make comparisons more robust. Despite these issues, the expected value to the community is high, and the work is likely to have significant impact by catalyzing further algorithmic progress and enabling reproducibility at scale. I recommend acceptance, conditional on clarifying the DPT claims, adding canonical splits and recipes, and strengthening the evaluation and ablations where feasible.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_093.assessment:
This paper tackles an important and timely problem: building a generalist, conversational VLM for temporal EO imagery. The contributions are primarily in dataset design (TEOChatlas), a practical and effective prompting strategy for temporal grounding, and a thorough empirical study demonstrating clear gains over strong baselines (Video-LLaVA, GeoChat), competitive performance with several specialists on select tasks, and robust zero-shot generalization. While algorithmic novelty is moderate—the architecture closely follows LLaVA/Video-LLaVA with a new prompt scheme—the work’s breadth, careful ablations, and open-source release make it a valuable community contribution. The main limitations are (i) reliance on axis-aligned box outputs that cap localization fidelity relative to segmentation SOTA, (ii) limited explicit temporal modeling in the vision backbone, and (iii) incomplete positioning with respect to closely related concurrent work on multi-temporal EO assistants (e.g., DVL‑Suite/DVLChat). Addressing these issues—particularly by clarifying evaluation parity, adding robustness analyses, and situating claims versus concurrent multi-temporal EO VLMs—would strengthen the paper. Overall, I find the paper solid and impactful, with a compelling dataset, a sensible and effective design, and strong empirical validation; I recommend acceptance after addressing the above clarifications and related-work positioning.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_005.assessment:
This work tackles an important and timely problem—building targeted, high-quality multimodal supervision at scale—through an engaging and potentially scalable gamified system. The deployment scale and the reCAPTCHA-inspired calibration are appealing, and the large in-domain improvement suggests the collected data can be impactful for the base model. However, the current methodology and evaluation leave significant gaps: the probabilistic estimation of informative samples is based on strong, under-validated assumptions; there is no modeling of annotator reliability or item difficulty; the internal evaluation relies on LLM-as-judge without human corroboration; and the cross-benchmark results are small and inconsistent, in tension with the paper’s generalization claims. Ethical and robustness aspects of the incentive design and telemetry are also insufficiently detailed.
Overall, I view the paper as a promising system/dataset contribution that requires stronger methodological grounding and substantially more rigorous evaluation. With principled annotator/item modeling, human evaluation of data quality, solid ablations, and clearer cross-benchmark gains (ideally on fine-grained hallucination diagnostics like FREAK or FINER), this line of work could be impactful. In its current form, I recommend rejection, with encouragement to strengthen the technical foundations and evaluation for a future submission.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_015.assessment:
This paper delivers a technically strong and conceptually insightful contribution: an exact finite-width solution of a non-convex, two-layer linear network (with 1D data) that cleanly separates kernel and feature-learning dynamics, codifies alignment/disalignment/rescaling mechanisms, and yields actionable phase diagrams in terms of scaling exponents. While the solvability relies on restrictive assumptions (linear network, 1D data, gradient flow), the clarity of the resulting insights—especially the role of initialization vs learning-rate/output-scale balance and the monotone alignment conditions—is compelling. Empirical validations are mostly qualitative but consistent; adding quantitative NTK measurements, SGD robustness checks, and clearer positioning against µP/DMFT/NTH frameworks would strengthen the case. Overall, I see this as a valuable theoretical paper with practical implications; despite the scope limitations, the novelty and clarity justify acceptance. I recommend a weak accept, contingent on tightening empirical validations and clarifying a few claims/terminology.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_017.assessment:
This paper presents a well-executed and practically meaningful advance: a rectified-flow vocoder that operates at the frame level, generates complex spectrograms with multi-band parallelism, and reaches high quality in only 10 steps. The combination of architectural choices (ConvNeXtV2 backbone, subband-aware conditioning), tailored objectives (energy-balanced, overlap, STFT losses), and a trajectory-aware time discretization (“equal straightness”) is thoughtful and empirically validated. The experimental section is broad and generally convincing, with ablations that isolate the contribution of each component and extensive speed benchmarks showing that RFWave is one of the fastest diffusion-type vocoders to date while retaining strong robustness across domains. Some comparisons could be made fairer by better aligning training data across baselines, and certain method details (e.g., straightness scheduling) deserve more prominence in the main text. Related work coverage could be expanded to situate RFWave among recent rectified/flow-matching audio models and frame-level vocoders. Still, the paper’s contributions are substantial and likely to be valuable to the community. I lean toward acceptance, contingent on clarifying the discretization schedule, fortifying the MOS protocol details, and, if feasible, tightening baseline training parity in the camera-ready.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_023.assessment:
This paper makes a timely and impactful contribution by bringing instruction-following audio–language modeling to bioacoustics and by releasing a broader, more challenging benchmark (BEANS-Zero). The empirical results are strong across many zero-shot tasks, and the demonstration of positive transfer from speech/music to bioacoustics is convincing and practically important. While the architectural novelty is moderate (a careful adaptation of existing LALM components), the domain-specific engineering, dataset curation, and evaluation breadth are significant. The main reservations concern fairness and transparency in evaluation (string-matching and prompt alignment may unduly favor the proposed model over generic LALMs) and the absence of some competitive adaptation/probing baselines from recent literature. With clarifications and additional controlled comparisons, this work would be a valuable resource for the community. I lean toward a weak accept given the significance, open releases, and empirical strength, contingent on resolving evaluation ambiguities and strengthening baselines.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_029.assessment:
This paper presents a timely, simple, and empirically effective approach to mitigating copyright-infringing regurgitation via adaptive model fusion. The key idea—adaptive history-aware reweighting of base model logits to “balance” sequence likelihoods—is well-motivated and supported by both a theoretical lemma and consistent empirical evidence across text and code. The approach preserves downstream utility and composes with training-time mitigations, which are appealing properties for practitioners. The main limitations lie in assumptions (separability), scope (small, overfitted models rather than large, realistic deployments), lack of runtime characterization, and missing comparisons to other modern inference-time mitigations (e.g., activation steering). Despite these gaps, the work advances the conversation on practical, post-hoc copyright safeguards and would be of interest to the ICLR community. I lean toward a weak accept, contingent on clarifying the optimization details, reporting inference overhead, and, if possible, adding a sensitivity analysis to separability violations or a comparison to alternative inference-time defenses.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_035.assessment:
This paper presents a well-motivated, effective, and practical framework that leverages GNNs as strong multi-hop retrievers and LLMs as flexible readers for KGQA. The method delivers clear gains on two standard benchmarks, especially for multi-hop and multi-entity questions, while reducing reliance on expensive LLM retrieval. The contributions are more systems-oriented than theoretically deep; the theorem mainly provides intuition and the novelty is in the careful, modular integration and retrieval augmentation. Some evaluation aspects could be strengthened (significance, broader KGs, more recent retriever comparisons), and additional path-quality controls might further improve precision under tight token budgets. Nonetheless, the work is timely and valuable to the ICLR community given the surge of interest in RAG over structured knowledge and in hybrid GNN–LLM pipelines. I recommend acceptance.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_041.assessment:
This paper introduces a thoughtful and practically motivated extension to projection-based continual learning by explicitly stabilizing sample gradients—a signal closely tied to adversarial robustness—alongside the more common activation subspaces. Empirically, DGP consistently preserves robustness better than standard CL+defense hybrids, especially under stronger attacks and longer task sequences, with reasonable but noticeable costs in plasticity and overhead. The work is timely and valuable for bringing robustness considerations into rehearsal-free continual learning.
That said, several aspects need strengthening to meet top-tier standards: clearer and more transparent robustness evaluation settings (explicit ε in the main text), ablations that disentangle the contribution of the second (gradient) basis, analysis of compute/memory overheads, and an assessment in more constrained backbones (e.g., shared first layer) to confirm generality. The theoretical justification remains heuristic and would benefit from even partial formalization or controlled stress tests of the weak-constraint surrogate. Despite these caveats, the idea is novel and the results are promising. I lean toward a weak accept, contingent on clarifications and additional ablations; even if not all are feasible within page limits, improved reporting and targeted experiments would significantly elevate the paper.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_047.assessment:
The paper addresses an important and underexplored weakness of standard INRs—poor extrapolation on sequential/periodic signals—by introducing a principled architectural bias: a learnable Fourier mapping for the temporal coordinate and a multiplicative periodic×scale factorization. The approach is intuitive, well-motivated, and broadly applicable across scientific and time-series domains. Empirically, NeRT shows strong extrapolation on synthetic ODE/PDE tasks and competitive to superior results on several real datasets, and the ablation on learnable vs. fixed Fourier features substantiates the core design choice.
That said, there are notable gaps. The fairness of comparisons to strong time-series baselines is questionable given small input windows for very long horizons, and the lack of quantitative metrics in the PDE extrapolation weakens that section. The decomposition prior may be overly restrictive for more complex or nonstationary settings, and the theoretical justification in the main text is brief. Important related directions—learnable positional encodings beyond fixed Fourier features, discrete encodings (NB2E), and decomposition-centric deep forecasting—are not fully engaged with empirically or in discussion.
Overall, I view NeRT as a promising and practically useful INR design for periodic signals with meaningful extrapolation benefits. With stronger and more carefully controlled evaluations against state-of-the-art time-series baselines (and additional quantitative PDE results), plus clarifications on the learnable Fourier layer and decomposition efficacy, this work would make a solid contribution to the ICLR community.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_099.assessment:
This paper makes a compelling and timely contribution by introducing SRB, a comprehensive and extensible robustness benchmark for ASR that unifies a diverse set of controlled perturbations with clear evaluation protocols. The breadth of scenarios (including digital effects and adversarial attacks), multilingual coverage, and in-depth analyses across model families and subgroups make this resource valuable to the community. While some methodological choices (MOS-based normalization) and reporting details (attack configurations, decoding settings, statistical confidence) need clarification and could be strengthened, these are largely addressable. Given the benchmark’s potential to standardize and accelerate robustness research in ASR—and the practical insights surfaced by the empirical study—I view this work as a strong contribution suitable for ICLR publication, contingent on clarifications and minor revisions.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_027.assessment:
This paper addresses an important and timely problem—how to make diffusion models generate physically consistent dynamics—by presenting a coherent set of principles and practical recipes for integrating invariance and feasibility priors. The conceptual taxonomy around Jensen’s gap and the targeted guidance on when to use noise vs. data matching are useful takeaways. Empirical results on synthetic PDEs and particle systems support the claim that appropriately handled priors can improve physical plausibility and errors.
However, the current version falls short of top-tier standards in two respects: (i) empirical validation is limited (synthetic datasets, modest gains on PDEs) and lacks comparisons to closely related physics-informed diffusion baselines and equivariant architectures; (ii) some theoretical components (Theorem 1’s conditions, Theorem 3’s scope, ECM formalization) are either standard, only partially novel, or insufficiently substantiated in the main text. The reparameterization strategy for general nonlinear constraints is promising but under-specified with respect to inference-time guarantees.
Overall, I view this paper as a solid step toward principled physics-aware diffusion, with practical guidance that could benefit the community. Strengthening baselines, broadening datasets (including OOD/generalization tests), clarifying theoretical assumptions, and concretely enforcing constraints at sampling would substantially raise the impact. I recommend a borderline rejection at this stage, with the expectation that an improved version could be competitive for a top-tier venue.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_059.assessment:
This paper presents a well-motivated, practical, and generally effective decoding-time method that leverages mechanistic insights about retrieval heads to construct a principled contrastive partner. The approach is simple, training-free, and yields consistent improvements, with the strongest gains on context-grounded faithfulness and multi-hop reasoning tasks. While some baselines remain stronger in specific niches (e.g., CAD on NQ-Swap, ITI on TruthfulQA-Gen), DeCoRe’s breadth of applicability and competitive performance make it valuable. The main areas for improvement are (i) deeper analysis of sensitivity (retrieval-head selection and α calibration), (ii) runtime/memory profiling, and (iii) clearer baseline configuration details and additional ablations front-and-center. Overall, I find the contribution novel enough and empirically solid for ICLR, and I recommend acceptance after addressing the noted concerns.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_053.assessment:
This paper puts forward a simple yet conceptually strong idea: serialize 3D molecules into SE(3)-invariant discrete sequences and let generic LMs do the heavy lifting. The formulation is appealing, and the conditional generation results on QM9 are striking, suggesting that long-context sequence models can integrate property constraints very effectively when provided with a faithful, invariant 3D serialization. However, several important details—especially around canonical labeling for 3D point clouds, automorphism handling, and robustness of the anchor-based global frame—are underspecified and raise concerns about determinism and stability. The unconditional results are solid on QM9 but less compelling on GEOM-DRUGS; uniqueness is notably lower than diffusion baselines on QM9. Finally, the conditional evaluation hinges entirely on a learned property predictor, making it hard to assess true control accuracy without some oracle validation or at least validity-conditioned reporting. Overall, I find the representational contribution significant and the empirical signal promising, but I would like to see clarifications and additional evidence to fully endorse the strength of the claims. With stronger methodological clarity on canonicalization, a determinism guarantee, and a small amount of oracle-backed validation for conditional tasks, this work would be a valuable and timely contribution to ICLR.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_039.assessment:
This paper presents a compelling, timely, and practically important finding: a minimal tense transformation reliably bypasses refusal training across many state-of-the-art LLMs. The study is well-executed, with multiple models and judges, meaningful category analyses, and an initial defense exploration that surfaces real trade-offs. While the method is conceptually simple and the novelty primarily empirical rather than algorithmic, the breadth and consistency of the phenomenon, together with its direct implications for red-teaming and alignment practice, make the contribution significant. The main areas for improvement are methodological refinements—decoding-sensitivity analysis, minimal-edit ablations isolating tense from historical framing, severity-weighted evaluation, and open-weight replication for the fine-tuning defense. Despite these gaps, the work would be valuable to the ICLR community and to practitioners focused on LLM safety. I recommend acceptance, ideally with revisions addressing the aforementioned concerns.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_071.assessment:
This paper presents a timely and practically important benchmark that isolates a neglected but central capability for language agents: stateful, multi-file refactoring in large repositories. The combination of curated multi-file tasks, instruction variants, and AST-based subtests enables nuanced diagnosis of failure modes that other benchmarks blur. The empirical findings—especially the localization failures despite descriptive prompts, the downsides of strict linting in refactoring workflows, and context flooding-induced drift—are valuable to the community. The proposed state-aware interface is simple yet effective, yielding meaningful gains, and the POMDP framing plus “state update policy” notion connects this line of work to broader agent research.
The main reservations pertain to behavioral validity (AST subtests alone do not ensure semantic correctness), limited baseline diversity and ablations (particularly around alternative memory and summarization strategies), and a modest dataset size that is Python-only. While the state-aware idea is not conceptually new, its application and empirical validation in this setting are useful and actionable. On balance, I find the benchmark and analysis to be of clear value to the ICLR community studying agents, with room for strengthening via additional ablations and light-weight behavioral checks. I lean toward acceptance given the importance of the problem, the clarity of insights, and the potential for RefactorBench to catalyze progress on stateful reasoning in language agents.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_011.assessment:
This paper targets an important and practical problem: learning effective policies when neither a perfect reward nor optimal human supervision is available. ICoPro is a sensible, modular combination of standard ingredients—margin-based corrective imitation, TD learning under proxy rewards, and pseudo-label consistency—organized in an iterative two-phase loop. The experimental study is extensive and shows consistent advantages over strong baselines across diverse domains, with useful robustness analyses and an initial human study. The main reservation is novelty: the approach is close in spirit to DQfD-style IL+RL and to policy-regularization techniques; the pseudo-label margin and explicit phase separation, while effective, are incremental. Some evaluation choices (simulated labelers trained on rewards akin to proxies; small human study) and missing comparisons to recent IL+RL hybrids slightly weaken the case. Overall, I view this as a solid, practically relevant contribution with convincing empirical support and decent clarity; with strengthened baselines and a more rigorous human-alignment evaluation, it would make a strong ICLR paper. I lean toward a weak accept.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_077.assessment:
This paper makes a substantive and original contribution by extending µP to local-learning algorithms and uncovering qualitative phenomena (PC’s gradient interpolation via γ_L and TP’s lack of a kernel regime). The theoretical developments are sound within their stated assumptions and are reinforced by focused experiments that demonstrate µTransfer and connect predictions to observed behavior. The main limitations are in empirical breadth (simple datasets/architectures), reliance on linear-feedback assumptions for TP, and first-step/linear-network analyses for the most explicit results. Despite these, the work addresses an important and timely problem—scaling local learning with principled hyperparameter transfer—and provides new theoretical tools and intuitions that are likely to be valuable to the ICLR community. I lean toward acceptance provided the authors strengthen the empirical section (e.g., add CIFAR-10/100 and a deeper CNN, and more DTP results) and clarify assumptions and notation.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_089.assessment:
This is a strong empirical study that advances our understanding of what actually helps LLMs “reason” in multi-turn code generation under realistic compute constraints. The paper’s main contributions are diagnostic rather than algorithmic, but they are carefully designed, well-validated across models/datasets, and yield actionable insights: combine reasoning+instruction prompts; favor adaptive CoT (CoT-retry); prefer simpler execution feedback to preserve exploratory diversity at scale; and use RFT on multi-turn CoT to internalize these benefits. The compute-aware evaluation (pass n@k) and the analysis of exploration–exploitation induced by feedback granularity are particularly valuable. While novelty is moderate and token-level compute is not fully normalized, the breadth and clarity of findings, plus the demonstrated generalization to TACO, make this work a worthwhile contribution for ICLR. I recommend acceptance.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_095.assessment:
This paper addresses an important and timely problem—making audio deepfake detectors more explainable and robust across domains—and surfaces a practically relevant observation: naive occlusion on spectrogram-based transformers can spuriously flag padding as most “important.” The cross-dataset evaluation is directionally appropriate, and the qualitative attention-rollout visualizations are informative. However, methodological novelty is modest (adaptations of standard explainability tools), the proposed “benchmark” offers limited added value beyond a simple two-dataset transfer test, and the experimental evaluation lacks standard metrics, strong baselines, and statistical rigor needed for a top-tier venue. The absence of quantitative validation of explanation faithfulness further weakens the central explainability claim. I recommend rejection in its current form, with the suggestion to (i) adopt standard EER/min-tDCF metrics with rigorous baselines (WavLM/AASIST/HierCon/DeepFense recipes), (ii) expand cross-domain evaluation to modern multilingual and perturbed benchmarks (XMAD-Bench, P2V), (iii) formalize and validate the explainability framework with fidelity metrics and/or user studies, and (iv) thoroughly document and release the benchmark protocol and code.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_007.assessment:
This is a strong, practically impactful paper with a clean idea: build large-scale synthetic ranked preference datasets with reward ensembles and train with a listwise-weighted diffusion-DPO objective. The empirical gains are consistent across multiple benchmarks and a sizable user study, and the approach is both compute- and data-efficient. While the listwise weighting is not a fundamentally new learning principle, the adaptation to diffusion-DPO and the practical Syn-Pic pipeline together form a valuable contribution likely to be influential in T2I alignment practice.
That said, there are notable concerns that should be addressed to solidify the case: potential evaluator leakage due to overlapping training and evaluation metrics (VQAScore), limited statistical detail for the human study, missing comparisons/discussion with closely related recent ranking/curriculum/safeguarded-DPO approaches, and lack of evaluation on drift-resistant benchmarks like GenEval 2. These are addressable and do not detract from the central merit of the work.
Overall, I lean toward acceptance. The paper presents a scalable, well-executed recipe that improves two widely used T2I models and offers clear ablations and practical insights. With clarifications and a few additional controls (especially removing metric leakage), this work would be a valuable addition to ICLR.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_001.assessment:
Unisolver is a well-motivated and carefully engineered conditional Transformer that leverages a thoughtful decomposition of PDE information (domain-wise vs. point-wise) and injects it via decoupled conditioning to attain strong cross-PDE generalization. The empirical results across three sizable and diverse benchmarks are compelling, and the ablations/analyses substantiate the design choices. While some claims (e.g., “first universal Transformer”) are overstated in the current landscape, the paper makes a meaningful contribution to building generalizable PDE surrogates by showing that explicit, structured conditioning on a more complete set of PDE components can materially improve performance over scale-alone strategies. The main areas to strengthen before publication are (i) physics-aware diagnostics and long-horizon stability analyses, (ii) fairer or broader comparisons to recent transformer operators and physics-foundation models, and (iii) deeper discussion of robustness to component errors and the practicality of LLM-based symbolic embeddings. Overall, I find the paper significant and recommend acceptance after addressing these points.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_025.assessment:
This paper tackles an important and timely question: formal stability, convergence, and robustness properties of predictive coding networks, and their relationship to second-order optimization. The high-level narrative is compelling and the empirical results suggest that PC can be competitive in accuracy and sometimes reaches its best validation performance in fewer epochs than BP/TP. However, the theoretical contributions are currently too high-level to be convincing for a top-tier venue: theorems lack precise statements and constants; some claims (exponential rates, “faster than BP” in general, quasi-Newton closeness vs TP) require substantially stronger assumptions or careful derivations that are not provided; and there is confusion between state- and parameter-level dynamics. On the empirical side, efficiency claims omit per-epoch inference costs and wall-clock/energy comparisons, and the robustness narrative is not tested experimentally. With stronger, explicit mathematics (including correct conditions for rates), tighter alignment between implemented algorithms and theoretical forms, and a more thorough empirical evaluation (compute-aware efficiency and robustness ablations), this work could become a significant contribution. In its current form, I recommend rejection, with encouragement to revise along the lines above.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_013.assessment:
The paper explores a compelling direction for FRL: federating a behaviorally meaningful state encoder trained via an approximated RAG distance, with periodic aggregation and a proximal regularizer to navigate heterogeneity. The approach is conceptually clean, communication-efficient, and empirically promising across several DMC tasks, including robustness to background distractors. However, the technical novelty is moderate—largely a thoughtful recombination of prior ideas (RAG/MICo with FedAvg/FedProx-style aggregation)—and several aspects need strengthening for ICLR: a more rigorous privacy treatment, stronger and more appropriate baselines (FedProx, personalized FL, heterogeneity-aware FRL, full-model FedAvg), clearer experimental protocol (seeds, significance, communication costs), and resolution of notation/clarity issues. With these additions and a deeper empirical comparison against state-of-the-art federated and representation-learning baselines, the contribution could offer substantial value. In its current form, I lean toward rejection, but I see a clear path to a strong paper with the above improvements.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_019.assessment:
This paper presents a focused and well-executed contribution: a simple span-aware finetuning algorithm that directly exploits MQM span annotations to improve MT. The span-level unlikelihood loss is an elegant mechanism to let the model decide which token conditionals to suppress within an error span, and the trajectory-aware handling of non-error tokens is both intuitive and empirically impactful. The experimental evidence is strong on En→De and solid on Zh→En, with thorough ablations and a fair DPO comparison. The main limitations are the absence of human evaluation, the lack of a token-level unlikelihood baseline to fully validate the span-level choice, and incomplete analysis of when to ignore off-trajectory tokens. Despite these gaps, the work is technically sound, timely, and pragmatic, and I expect it to be valuable to researchers working on learning from fine-grained feedback. I recommend acceptance, with encouragement to add the requested ablations, human evaluation, and additional diagnostics in the camera-ready.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_037.assessment:
The paper presents a conceptually simple and potentially useful idea: using OT-based, one-shot color/style alignment as a preprocessing step to reduce cross-client heterogeneity in FL. However, the current experimental evidence is not credible—reported CIFAR-10 test accuracies near 99% with a small CNN and few rounds are implausible and suggest methodological issues such as data leakage or non-standard evaluation. Moreover, the heterogeneity setting appears iid rather than non-iid, key implementation details of the projection are omitted, and critical baselines (including known data-level DG for FL like CCST and classic color-alignment methods) are missing. As written, the central claims are not adequately supported, and the novelty is limited to applying known OT color-transfer ideas to an FL preprocessing context. I encourage the authors to (i) rigorously define the projection step, (ii) adopt standard non-iid FL benchmarks and fair, in-house baselines, (iii) provide thorough ablations and alignment diagnostics, and (iv) address privacy considerations. With substantial revision and rigorous evaluation, the method could become a lightweight tool complementary to existing FL algorithms, but the current version is not ready for a top-tier venue.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_033.assessment:
This paper presents a compelling, generally applicable adaptive-compute framework for linear layers and a practical instantiation (RaNA) that unifies input-conditional low-rank adaptation across MLP and attention projections. Conceptually, the re-framing from neurons to ranks is clean and useful; technically, the activation-aware A,B construction and simple B-masker are well-motivated; and empirically, the method shows consistent improvements over strong adaptive baselines on modern architectures, with realized latency gains. The main weaknesses are (i) some sloppiness in the theoretical exposition (AB vs. ABX), (ii) limited fairness controls when comparing across different layer coverage, and (iii) missing comparisons against recent activation-aware low-rank/static baselines and more extensive latency analyses. Overall, I view this as a solid and practically relevant contribution. With clarifications on theory, added ablations to isolate contributions, and broader baselines, it would be a strong fit for ICLR.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_043.assessment:
This paper raises a timely and practically significant point: ℓ1-penalized likelihood can fundamentally misrepresent sparsity in linear Gaussian DAG learning when the task is to identify the sparsest member of an equivalence class, and this failure persists even at global optima. The empirical evidence via permutation/Cholesky enumeration is compelling. The proposed CALM method, which integrates an ℓ0-style penalty, hard acyclicity, and a moral-graph prior, is a sensible and effective remedy in practice, delivering strong improvements over GOLEM-NV-ℓ1 and NOTEARS and robust behavior under standardization. The main limitations are (i) the lack of a formal theorem for the inconsistency claim; (ii) reliance on a strong prior (moral graph) without fully controlled fairness tests; (iii) missing comparisons to relevant differentiable methods with nonconvex penalties and to DAGMA; and (iv) no runtime/scalability analysis. With additional theoretical grounding, ablations that equalize priors across baselines, broader comparisons, and computational reporting, this work could make a strong contribution. In its current form, I lean toward a weak reject for ICLR, primarily due to the overstatement of the ℓ1 “inconsistency” without formal backing and the fairness gaps in evaluation; nonetheless, the ideas and empirical insights are valuable, and I encourage a revision that addresses these points.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_067.assessment:
The paper tackles an important and timely problem—scalable, high-fidelity time-series generation for mobility, with a clever conditional scheme that deaggregates spatial marginals into trajectories and promises OOD adaptability without retraining. The approach is conceptually sound and practically appealing, and the breadth of experiments is commendable. However, several issues limit confidence: key implementation details are missing, evaluation of the conditional mode overemphasizes the same marginals used as inputs (risking tautological validation), trajectory-level realism and physical feasibility are not rigorously assessed, and comparisons omit several highly relevant recent baselines. Some metrics appear unstable, and efficiency claims are not fully substantiated. I view this as a promising direction with solid potential, but it requires stronger empirical rigor, clearer methodological specification, and fairer, more comprehensive comparisons—especially for the mobility setting—before it reaches the bar of a top-tier venue. I encourage the authors to address these points; with the suggested additions and clarifications, the work could be quite impactful.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_083.assessment:
This paper tackles an important and underexplored problem: robust barycenter estimation in the continuous setting with general costs. The main contributions—a semi-dual SUOT-barycenter formulation with m-congruence, theoretically grounded links between potentials and plan marginals, and a neural training procedure with a recovery bound—are technically sound and novel. The empirical section clearly demonstrates intended behavior on synthetic data and provides a compelling qualitative general-cost application on a learned manifold, with released code for reproducibility. The primary weaknesses lie in the limited scope of large-scale quantitative experiments, incomplete discussion of related concurrent (Gaussian SUOT) barycenter work, and practical under-specification of the weak c-transform approximation and inference overhead. Additionally, no mitigation is proposed for known spurious solutions in minimax neural OT training. Overall, I find the core idea and theory promising and relevant to the ICLR community. Strengthening the empirical evaluation (quantitative high-dimensional results, ablations over ψ/τ, runtime/scalability), clarifying inference details, and expanding related-work discussion would substantially improve the paper. I lean towards borderline accept given the novelty and potential impact, contingent on addressing the noted gaps.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_073.assessment:
This paper presents a compelling, principled, and practically effective approach to inducing simple, compressible behaviors in RL by maximizing trajectory total correlation through a tractable variational lower bound. The methodological contribution is clear and novel: jointly shaping latent state and action sequences via history-based predictors provides a powerful inductive bias that translates into stronger robustness and, in many cases, higher nominal performance. The experimental evaluation is broad and careful (many seeds, confidence intervals, multiple benchmarks and perturbations), and the qualitative and quantitative evidence (trajectory plots, compression, prediction errors) tightly aligns with the core hypothesis.
The main limitations are the vacuousness of the bound for estimation, the theoretical–practical gap introduced by off-policy predictor training, and some missing robustness baselines/perturbations and sensitivity analyses. Nonetheless, the empirical gains and the conceptual clarity make a strong case for acceptance. I believe the work will be valuable to the community as a general, domain-agnostic regularization principle and as a lens on sequence-level information in RL. I recommend acceptance, with suggestions to expand sensitivity studies, report compute overheads, and broaden robustness comparisons in the camera-ready.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_079.assessment:
This paper addresses an important and timely question: whether FP8 (and sub‑BF16) training is not just feasible but robust and cost-effective for LLMs. The authors offer a useful perspective by emphasizing stability, hyperparameter sensitivity, and dataset effects rather than raw throughput. Their bit-reduction emulation and the proposed last-token logit sharpness metric are interesting contributions; the empirical evidence that sharpness rises before divergence and that exponent bits are pivotal is consistent with prior observations and valuable for practitioners. However, the experimental methodology falls short of conclusively supporting the strongest claims. The emulation departs from real FP8 pipelines in material ways; the sharpness metric lacks rigorous predictive validation against competing diagnostics; long-horizon evidence is scarce; and comparisons to newer, more robust FP8 recipes (FOG, InfiR2) are missing. Overall, I view the paper as a promising initial study with practical insights, but it requires a stronger experimental foundation, more comprehensive FP8 baselines, and quantitative predictive validation to meet the ICLR bar. I lean toward rejection in its current form, with encouragement to resubmit after addressing the above points.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_085.assessment:
This paper targets an important and timely problem: improving PPO’s trust-region behavior and stability with a simple, first-order objective modification. The proposed SPO loss is easy to implement, has intuitive per-sample behavior, and yields promising empirical improvements, particularly with deeper policies where PPO often becomes brittle. These practical merits are the paper’s main strength.
However, the theoretical contributions are weaker than claimed: showing that TV-based lower bounds can be larger than KL-based ones does not translate into guarantees for the proposed algorithm under function approximation, nor does the paper demonstrate that the TV trust region is enforced in expectation. The chosen penalty form, while intuitively motivated, is not derived from the constrained problem and lacks a principled dual formulation. On the empirical side, several methodological concerns (implausible ratio deviations, non-standard normalization, limited seeds on Atari, missing ablations and key baselines) reduce confidence in the breadth and robustness of the reported gains.
Overall, I view SPO as a potentially useful and practical alternative to PPO with encouraging early results, but the paper in its current form overstates the theoretical strength and leaves important experimental questions open. With stronger diagnostics, fairer and broader comparisons, sensitivity studies, and a more careful theoretical connection to constraint satisfaction, this work could have significant practical impact. I recommend a borderline rejection at this stage, with encouragement to revise and resubmit.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_091.assessment:
This paper presents a thoughtful and principled approach to activation-aware low-rank compensation for W4A4 PTQ, with clear derivations and solid empirical gains over QuaRot and simple SVD-based corrections. The idea to explicitly separate roles—quantized weights act on quantized activations while the FP16 low-rank term acts on unquantized activations—and to solve for the low-rank path using activation statistics is technically compelling. The work is relevant and timely.
However, the paper does not yet establish a comprehensive state-of-the-art position. Several highly relevant and recent W4A4 methods (KurTail, SmoothRot, ASER, SERQ) are not compared, and practical deployment implications (latency, memory, kernel fusion) are insufficiently characterized. The FP16 low-rank overhead at 10–30% “matrix-equivalent rank” budgets is non-trivial, and the observed limited benefit of multiple alternations suggests a simpler one-shot procedure may suffice. Addressing these issues—by adding stronger baselines, tighter runtime/memory analysis, and possibly exploring lower-overhead compensators—would substantially strengthen the contribution.
On balance, I view this as a promising submission with solid technical content and encouraging results but with empirical and practical gaps that make it borderline for ICLR. With additional comparisons and deployment-oriented evaluation, it could be a strong paper.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_097.assessment:
This paper makes a clear, well-motivated, and practically valuable contribution: identifying a specific shortcoming in NPO and proposing a simple, reference-free alternative (SimNPO) that consistently improves unlearning–utility trade-offs across standard benchmarks. The technical ideas are modest but insightful—especially the diagnosis of reference model bias and the gradient-weight analysis—and the empirical evidence is strong relative to the core baseline (NPO), including useful analyses of early-stage behavior and relearning robustness. The main weaknesses are limited comparisons to several closely related, concurrent methods (DiPO, CATNIP, adaptive-margin variants) and the lack of adversarial white-box robustness evaluation; privacy gains on MUSE, while improved, are still far from retrain. Despite these gaps, the work is timely, easy to adopt, and likely to influence practice. I lean toward acceptance, contingent on strengthening baselines/discussion and, if possible, adding a white-box robustness evaluation.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_065.assessment:
This paper presents a clear and useful perspective on LoRA as full fine-tuning with constrained, low-rank gradients and turns that insight into a practical per-step gradient adjustment with closed-form updates. The theoretical development is clean, the algorithm is simple to implement on top of existing LoRA code, and the empirical gains are convincing across NLP and vision settings with modest overhead. The primary reservations are (i) somewhat overstated novelty regarding the equivalence principle vis-à-vis prior work on gradient transformations and rank-r step projection, (ii) missing direct comparisons to the most closely related step-projection/preconditioning methods (e.g., PSI-LORA/LORSUM), and (iii) limited analysis of optimizer interactions and early-step rank-deficiency handling. Addressing these concerns—particularly by situating the contribution more precisely relative to recent theory and by adding a couple of targeted comparisons/ablations—would significantly strengthen the paper. Overall, I recommend acceptance (weak accept), as the method is well-motivated, broadly applicable, and likely to be valuable to the community.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_055.assessment:
This paper tackles an important and timely problem—making disagreement-based ensemble diversification practical at scale—by eliminating the need for auxiliary OOD data and addressing computational bottlenecks. The empirical results at ImageNet scale and the introduction of PDS as a diversity-aware OOD score are valuable contributions likely to interest the ICLR community. That said, the paper would benefit from clearer and more precise methodological exposition (notably the dynamic weighting derivation and complexity claims), more transparent reporting of the ID-vs-OOD tradeoff, and broader baseline comparisons for OOD detection. I view this as a solid, practical contribution with incremental novelty and strong empirical appeal; with the requested clarifications and strengthened comparisons, it would be competitive for acceptance.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_049.assessment:
This paper addresses an important and difficult problem—blind inverse problems—with a pragmatic EM framework that leverages the strong inductive bias of latent diffusion models. The proposed annealed consistency and skip-gradient strategies are sensible and yield tangible improvements in stability and efficiency, and the 2D blind deblurring results (especially kernel estimation) are promising. The exploratory extension to pose-free sparse-view 3D via multi-view LDM priors is an intriguing direction.
However, the novelty is moderate: the core ideas build on known DPS/EM constructs, and the stabilization/efficiency techniques are heuristic and similar in spirit to existing scheduling/skipping practices. More critically, the experimental evaluation omits some strong and highly relevant baselines (GibbsDDRM, BIRD, recent LDM-based blind methods like LADiBI), and the 3D validation is limited to qualitative examples without quantitative metrics, which weakens support for the broader claims. Clarity on the 3D optimization pathway is also lacking.
I see solid potential and practical value, especially if the authors strengthen comparisons and 3D quantification. As it stands, I view this as a borderline submission: valuable ideas and good early results, but requiring a more comprehensive empirical study and clearer positioning relative to closely related recent work to meet the bar for a top-tier venue.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_061.assessment:
This paper presents a compelling and timely contribution to code-as-policy robotics by demonstrating that large-scale, automatically synthesized program data from real robot videos can substantially lift zero-shot manipulation performance. The approach is conceptually simple yet effective: leverage a VLM for plan extraction and a code LLM for API-centric program synthesis, then train a multimodal code generator that bridges perception and execution. The empirical results are strong across two simulators and a real robot, with thoughtful evaluations of API robustness and unseen skills, plus informative ablations. The main reservations are around dataset quality auditing, missing comparisons to the most closely related multimodal code-generation frameworks, and limited discussion of safety and reproducibility. Still, the paper’s core idea and evidence are solid, and I expect the community would benefit from both the methodology and results. I recommend acceptance after addressing the clarifications above, ideally with a dataset/pipeline release or at least stronger data-quality analyses.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_031.assessment:
The paper presents an appealing, model-behavior-driven perspective on pre-selecting demonstrations and a unifying view that bridges ICL and fine-tuning. The LLM-conditioned sufficiency/necessity notions are conceptually interesting and, if backed by rigorous algorithms and comprehensive comparisons, could meaningfully influence data curation practice. However, the current submission has notable shortcomings: (i) the main approximation algorithm seems to operationalize sufficiency only, leaving necessity underutilized; (ii) the complexity analysis is inconsistent and likely underestimates cost; (iii) empirical evaluations omit several strong and highly relevant selection baselines, weakening claims of superiority; and (iv) parts of the theory rely on unrealistic assumptions (transitivity) or ill-defined objectives. Despite positive initial results and breadth of experiments, these gaps make it difficult to assess the robustness and practical advantage of FEEDER over existing methods. I recommend rejection at this stage, with encouragement to strengthen the methodology, complexity analysis, and comparisons. With these improvements, this line of work could be a solid contribution to ICLR.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review
